lesson.menu_title: First Conditional -- Owning the Room in an AI Safety Briefing
lesson.menu_desc: Build arguments and make predictions with if + will: governance, risk, and recommendations -- 34 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 4 -- First Conditional for Executive Presentations
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucia Nishiyama Serra | Lesson 4 -- First Conditional in Executive Presentations

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: the system of rules and decisions that controls how an organization is run
the act of following the official rules, laws, or standards for your work
a clear structure of rules and ideas you use to plan and decide
the amount of risk or possible harm you are open to
to make a risk or a problem less serious or less likely
the level at which something starts to happen or becomes acceptable
to make sure a rule or a policy is actually followed
to introduce a new policy or program step by step

reading.rtitle: How Leaders Use If ... Will to Own the Room
paras: In a high-level briefing, strong leaders do not only describe the problem; they show what will happen next. The first conditional -- if plus the present, will plus a verb -- is the language of prediction and recommendation. It lets you present a risk and, in the very same breath, offer a clear path forward. That is why boards trust it: it turns a worry into a decision.

Imagine a senior leader briefing the board on AI safety. She does not say 'maybe there is a problem.' She says: 'If we do not set a clear threshold, our exposure will grow. But if we enforce this framework, we will mitigate most of the risk.' Each sentence links a condition to a result. The board does not hear fear; it hears a plan. The tense itself sounds confident, because will is a promise, not a guess.

The same tool works for you in any presentation. When you want to sound decisive, connect a condition to a strong result with will. Use unless for a warning -- 'Unless leadership aligns, we will fall behind' -- and as long as for a promise -- 'As long as we stay compliant, we will keep our partners' trust.' Say it with will, and the room will listen.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- B1 reading, adapted from an executive communication guide

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: The first conditional is a difficult grammar rule that leaders should avoid in real briefings.
The first conditional lets a leader connect a condition to a result, turning a worry into a confident recommendation.
The text says leaders should only describe problems and never suggest what to do next.

items: The first conditional connects a condition to a result.
t
The text says: 'Each sentence links a condition to a result.'
A confident leader only describes the problem and never offers a path forward.
f
The text says strong leaders 'show what will happen next' and 'offer a clear path forward.'
You can use 'unless' to give a warning.
t
The text says: 'Use unless for a warning -- Unless leadership aligns, we will fall behind.'
In the first conditional, the result uses 'would' instead of 'will'.
f
The text uses 'will' in every result: 'we will mitigate', 'we will fall behind', 'the room will listen.'

parts: it. As long as the risk stays below the

gapfill.text: First conditional = If + present simple, will + base verb. Use it for real, likely futures: predictions, risks, and recommendations. After 'if', use the present -- never 'will'. 'Unless' means 'if ... not' (a warning). 'As long as' and 'provided that' also set a condition.

bank.label: Useful sentences for an executive briefing
items: If we implement this framework, we will reduce our exposure.
Unless leadership aligns on this, we won't be able to scale.
If you ask me about AI governance, I'll tell you why it matters now.
As long as we stay compliant, we will keep our partners' trust.
If the risk passes this threshold, we will pause the rollout.
Provided that the board approves, we will enforce it next quarter.
If we do nothing, our exposure will only grow.
If we act now, we will set the standard for the whole industry.

items.situation: Present one AI safety risk and your recommendation. Start with 'If we don't act...'.
tips: If we don't act, our exposure will grow.
Unless we mitigate this, we won't ...

items.situation: A board member asks how to reduce risk. Answer with a first conditional.
tips: If we enforce this framework, we will ...
As long as we ..., we will ...

items.situation: Make a prediction about AI governance in your company for the next year.
tips: If we set a clear threshold, ...

items.situation: Give a warning to leadership using 'unless'.
tips: Unless we act, our exposure will ...

items.situation: Make a promise to a partner using 'as long as'.
tips: As long as we stay compliant, we will ...
As long as we enforce this, ...

items: Round 1 -- Guided
In three sentences, present one AI safety risk and your recommendation. Use 'If ... we will ...' in each sentence.
Round 2 -- The mini-briefing
Give a one-minute briefing: one risk, one framework, one prediction. Use the first conditional at least three times.
Round 3 -- Unless and As long as
Deliver the same briefing again, but use 'unless' for a warning and 'as long as' for a promise.
Curveball
A skeptical board member says 'This will cost too much.' Respond with a first conditional that shows the cost of doing nothing.

answerkey.title: Reveal the model briefing
list: Model: 'If we do not set a clear threshold, our exposure will keep growing. But if we roll out this framework and enforce it, we will mitigate most of the risk. As long as we stay compliant, we will keep the board's trust. Unless leadership aligns now, we won't be able to scale safely next year.'
Prediction / risk: If we do nothing, our exposure will grow.
Recommendation: If we enforce this framework, we will reduce the risk.
Warning (unless): Unless we act, we will fall behind.
Promise (as long as): As long as we stay compliant, we will keep their trust.
answerkey.note: First conditional = If + present simple, will + base verb. It is for real, likely futures. After 'if', use the present, never 'will'. Use 'will' (not 'would') in the result to sound confident -- 'would' is the second conditional, for hypothetical situations.

listenings.text: Let me be direct about where we stand. Right now, our exposure is higher than it should be, and the reason is simple. We have a framework on paper, but we do not enforce it. So here is my recommendation. If we set a clear threshold today, we will know exactly when a project needs a second review. If we roll out the new controls across every team, we will mitigate most of the risk within one quarter. And if the board backs this plan, I will personally own the results. But I have to be honest with you. Unless we align on this now, our exposure will only grow, and by next year it will be much harder to fix. The good news is that the path is clear. As long as we act together, we will get ahead of this, not behind it.

Here is one small change that will transform how you sound in a briefing. Stop saying 'maybe' and 'I think there could be a problem.' Instead, use the first conditional. Say: if we do this, we will get that result. It is a tiny grammar shift, but it changes everything, because 'will' sounds like a decision, not a doubt. I have coached many senior leaders, and the ones who own the room are not the loudest. They are the ones who connect a clear condition to a clear result. So before your next presentation, write down three sentences in this shape. If we act, we will reduce the risk. Unless we align, we will fall behind. As long as we stay compliant, we will keep their trust. Practice them out loud until they feel natural. If you do that, the room will listen.

extra_audio.text: If we do not set a clear threshold, our exposure will grow. If we roll out this framework, we will mitigate most of the risk. Unless leadership aligns on this, we won't be able to scale. As long as we stay compliant, we will keep the board's trust. If we act now, we will set the standard for the whole industry.
